coredata.dc:title: Stress-informed transfer learning: Accelerated battery life evaluation model across diverse operating conditions and cell mechanisms
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Guangyuan
Du, Yuhang
Song, Yuchen
Liu, Datong
Peng, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Life evaluation for newly developed lithium-ion batteries is often constrained by the time-intensive and costly nature of battery testing. This is particularly true in the aerospace industry, where limited comprehensive data availability significantly hampers life evaluations. Data collected from batteries under diverse operating conditions and cell mechanisms provides valuable insights for constructing degradation models. Nevertheless, the nonlinearity in battery degradation across operating conditions, combined with data distribution discrepancies among different cell mechanisms, presents significant challenges in developing degradation models for newly designed batteries. In this study, a stress-informed transfer learning methodology is proposed to accelerate the life evaluation process. Firstly, a stochastic model is employed to capture the nonlinear dynamics inherent in battery degradation under diverse operating conditions. Model migration is implemented to adapt stochastic models to unique degradation trends, ensuring precision under varying stresses. Secondly, a Transformer-based model is developed to accommodate variations in data distributions across different cell mechanisms. Domain-adaptive fine-tuning with specified loss function is then incorporated to address the challenge of limited target degradation features. Finally, a hybrid model is devised by integrating these foundational components, realizing accelerated life evaluation through the utilization of multi-modal data. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed methodology achieves improvements of 63.40 % in MAE and 58.55 % in RMSE with 30 % training data length compared to mainstream benchmark methods. This highlights the method’s potential as an early-stage screening and assessment tool for newly developed space lithium-ion batteries, complementing conventional cycle life evaluation protocols with accelerated evaluations from limited degradation data.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Accelerated life evaluation
Transfer learning
Stochastic model
Transformer network, Model fusion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Battery degradation modeling across different operating conditions
GBM model parameter identification with high-stress battery data
Model migration with normal-stress battery data
Battery degradation modeling across different cell mechanisms
Transformer-based model training with historical model battery data
Domain adaptive fine-tuning with newly designed battery data
Fusion model development and life evaluation
Model fusion with particle filter algorithm
Degradation feature iterative prediction and life evaluation
Evaluation of life prediction performance across method types
Relations between prediction accuracy and training data length

Schematic framework of the proposed method for modeling batteries under different operating conditions.
Schematic framework of the proposed method for modeling batteries under different cell mechanisms.
Schematic diagram of fusion model development and life evaluation.

Prediction results for C1–1 across methods at varying training data lengths in ablation experiment.
Total training time for models at varying training data lengths.
Prediction results for C1–1 across methods at varying training data lengths in comparison experiment.
Cycle life prediction results comparison across method types (a) MAE (b) RMSE.
Cycle life prediction results comparison with different training data length in MAE metric; (a) MAE (b) ΔMAE (c) Amplified view of MAE values.

AE metric for C1–1 across methods at varying training data lengths in comparison experiment.
MAE and RMSE metrics for four target batteries across methods at varying training data lengths.

head.ce:title: Stress-informed transfer learning: Accelerated battery life evaluation model across diverse operating conditions and cell mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronics and Information Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronics and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Electronics and Information Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Space Power-Sources, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Space Power-Sources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Space Power-Sources, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Life evaluation for newly developed lithium-ion batteries is often constrained by the time-intensive and costly nature of battery testing. This is particularly true in the aerospace industry, where limited comprehensive data availability significantly hampers life evaluations. Data collected from batteries under diverse operating conditions and cell mechanisms provides valuable insights for constructing degradation models. Nevertheless, the nonlinearity in battery degradation across operating conditions, combined with data distribution discrepancies among different cell mechanisms, presents significant challenges in developing degradation models for newly designed batteries. In this study, a stress-informed transfer learning methodology is proposed to accelerate the life evaluation process. Firstly, a stochastic model is employed to capture the nonlinear dynamics inherent in battery degradation under diverse operating conditions. Model migration is implemented to adapt stochastic models to unique degradation trends, ensuring precision under varying stresses. Secondly, a Transformer-based model is developed to accommodate variations in data distributions across different cell mechanisms. Domain-adaptive fine-tuning with specified loss function is then incorporated to address the challenge of limited target degradation features. Finally, a hybrid model is devised by integrating these foundational components, realizing accelerated life evaluation through the utilization of multi-modal data. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed methodology achieves improvements of 63.40 % in MAE and 58.55 % in RMSE with 30 % training data length compared to mainstream benchmark methods. This highlights the method’s potential as an early-stage screening and assessment tool for newly developed space lithium-ion batteries, complementing conventional cycle life evaluation protocols with accelerated evaluations from limited degradation data.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The adoption of lithium-ion batteries in fields like electric vehicles, energy storage systems and aerospace industry has surged in recent years, attributed to their superior energy density, durability, low rate of self-discharge, and enhanced safety features. However, as operating batteries undergo constant charge and discharge cycles, they gradually degrade, leading to their end of life (EOL). This undermines the performance of power systems and can result in safety hazards. Therefore, life evaluation for lithium-ion batteries is of great significance, as it provides valuable insights into optimizing operating strategies and arranging scheduled maintenance in practical applications [1].

For lithium-ion batteries in the design phase, an accurate and efficient life evaluation can significantly accelerate the R&D cycle, while minimizing the need for time-consuming testing procedures [2]. Although there has been extensive research on various degradation mechanisms and predictive models, accurately evaluating the cycle life of newly developed lithium-ion batteries remains a major challenge in industrial scenarios. This is particularly evident in aerospace applications, where batteries deployed in satellites and deep-space missions are expected to operate reliably for over a decade [3,4]. In order to achieve life evaluations for such battery models with long cycle life, rigorous degradation testing is employed to collect comprehensive data for analytical modeling. These tests involve subjecting batteries to higher stress conditions, such as elevated temperatures or increased charge-discharge rates, in order to accelerate aging processes and collect degradation data within a reduced timeframe [5]. However, in specialized fields like the aerospace industry, short-term battery testing captures only the initial segments of battery degradation data. These initial segments of data often display no obvious degradation trends and provide limited feature on battery degradation, thereby presenting significant challenges for accelerated life evaluations [6].

Traditional modeling approaches, while advantageous in many scenarios, face substantial obstacles when data availability is limited. Both conventional model-based and data-driven approaches demand substantial volumes of high-quality, consistent data to accurately characterize degradation mechanisms [6–8]. This often necessitates extensive, long-term testing under controlled conditions, such as uniform cell batches and standardized cycling protocols. However, such comprehensive data is often scarce for newly developed batteries, making it challenging to accurately model their degradation behavior. Consequently, there is an urgent need for innovative modeling methodologies that address the challenges of data incompleteness, providing reliable cycle life evaluations for newly developed lithium-ion batteries.

When faced with data incompleteness in degradation modeling, alternative data sources can provide critical support in constructing robust evaluation frameworks [9]. In real industrial scenarios complete degradation data are available from two major sources. The first source is the same model batteries subjected to higher stress. Research indicates that batteries operating under higher stress conditions, such as high discharge rates [10,11], high SOC [12] and extreme temperatures [13,14], exhibit faster degradation trends. Therefore, when conducting degradation testing on both normal-condition and high-stress-condition batteries in parallel, the batteries under high-stress-condition will reach failure much faster. The second source is historical model batteries with similar cell mechanisms. In many cases, new battery systems are only slight improvements on the internal chemistry of previous generations. Consequently, similar cell mechanisms often exhibit comparable degradation characteristics [15]. Given the extensive utilization of historical model batteries, the complete degradation data collected during their use can also assist in degradation modeling for newly developed batteries.

Consequently, the key to achieving accelerated life evaluations for newly developed batteries lies in degradation modeling across diverse operating conditions and cell mechanisms. Modeling newly designed batteries under varying operating conditions presents a significant challenge, primarily due to the stochastic variability arising from diverse stress factors [16]. Stochastic process models offer a promising solution by capturing the inherent uncertainties in battery degradation, enabling precise modeling with limited sample data [17,18]. Despite its potential, the existing stochastic modeling approaches often underperform in cases where data incompleteness obscures discernible degradation trends. Model migration, a subset of transfer learning methodologies, offers a viable solution by effectuating minor adaptations to critical parameters within pre-existing models [19]. Tang et al. [20] demonstrated the feasibility of accurate aging trajectory predictions for batteries operating under normal-speed aging conditions by leveraging a migrated model based on data from accelerated aging samples. Zhou et al. [21] introduced a two-stage aging trajectory prediction method to forecast battery aging by integrating complete degradation segments from one specific battery cell into another subjected to different stress conditions. While existing studies have demonstrated decent modeling capabilities, they predominantly rely on empirical, model-based frameworks that do not account for the stochastic variability inherent in battery degradation. The integration of model migration techniques within stochastic modeling frameworks holds significant potential to address these gaps, facilitating precise adaptation to specific degradation trends. Currently, this promising yet underexplored area warrants further investigation.

Additionally, transfer learning with machine learning techniques represents a prominent approach for modeling across various cell mechanisms [22]. This method involves pre-training a model on source domain data and subsequently fine-tune the model parameters for improved performance in the target domain. Consequently, extensive data from a single chemistry type can be efficiently utilized for the modeling of batteries with different mechanisms. Kim et al. [23] employed fine-tuning strategy based on LSTM network between battery datasets with LCO and NMC chemistries and achieving decent cycle life prediction results. Ma et al. [24] explored the effectiveness and generalizability of employing fine-tuning between LFP and NMC batteries, acquiring accurate cycle life prediction for the target battery type. However, conventional fine-tuning strategies exhibit compromised effectiveness in scenarios where target degradation features are limited, particularly due to incomplete datasets, thereby constraining their efficacy in achieving accurate modeling across diverse mechanisms.

In summary, while existing studies have made significant advancements in battery degradation modeling across diverse operating conditions and cell mechanisms, achieving accelerated life evaluations for newly developed batteries still necessitates further enhancements building upon the foundation of existing research.
(1)
The influence of operating conditions on battery performance degradation is commonly modeled using stochastic process frameworks. However, existing stochastic process models exhibit reduced accuracy when clear degradation trends are absent. Their reliance on complete degradation data restricts their applicability in scenarios where only partial degradation data is available, thereby complicating the task of accurately modeling the degradation processes of newly designed batteries.
(2)
Transfer learning demonstrates promising potential for establishing degradation models across varying cell mechanisms. Nevertheless, conventional fine-tuning strategies in machine learning struggle to address the challenge posed by limited target degradation features. This limitation undermines modeling accuracy when applying models trained on one battery mechanism to predict the performance of batteries with distinct mechanisms.
(3)
Existing studies predominantly rely on single-source data, limiting model robustness and generalizability. Integrating multi-modal data allows for capturing a broader spectrum of operating conditions and cell mechanisms, thereby enhancing the comprehensiveness and accuracy of degradation modeling.

To address the aforementioned challenges, this paper proposes a stress-informed transfer learning methodology that combines stress-informed modeling with transfer learning techniques to account for diverse battery operating conditions and cell mechanisms. By integrating these two modeling approaches, the method aims to accelerate the life evaluation process for newly developed batteries. The main contributions of this paper are as follows:
(1)
This study introduces an approach to facilitate model migration within the context of stochastic process models. By leveraging complete degradation data from batteries subjected to higher stress conditions, the technique effectively adapts the model to specific degradation trends, thereby facilitating precise modeling of degradation processes across diverse operating conditions.
(2)
This study leverages domain-adaptive fine-tuning to address the challenge of limited degradation features. By reducing mismatches in data distribution across varied cell mechanisms through the application of a specified loss function, this approach significantly improves feature representation capabilities and achieves enhanced modeling performance across cell mechanisms.
(3)
This study proposes a novel approach that integrates degradation data from multiple sources, including high-stress-condition batteries in parallel testing and historical model batteries with minor variations in cell mechanisms. The two aforementioned modeling approaches are fused with a particle filter algorithm to yield accuracy cycle life prediction results for newly developed batteries with minimal training data length.

Compared with most existing methodologies for life prognosis and remaining useful life prediction, the novelty of this work can be summarized as follows:
(1)
Traditional battery degradation modeling approaches typically rely on single-source data and static methodologies, limiting their robustness and generalizability. To overcome these limitations, this study introduces a comprehensive stress-informed transfer learning framework. By leveraging multi-modal data, the proposed method improves the modeling process under incomplete data scenarios, thereby yielding more precise and reliable degradation models for newly developed batteries.
(2)
Unlike traditional stochastic process models that rely heavily on complete datasets, this study innovatively facilitates model migration, enabling the adaptation of stochastic models to partial degradation data. By capturing degradation dynamics under diverse operating conditions, the proposed methodology addresses data incompleteness while maintaining high fidelity in degradation predictions.
(3)
The paper advances conventional fine-tuning methods by incorporating domain-adaptive strategies that address the challenge of limited degradation features across cell mechanisms. This application of transfer learning allows for more efficient utilization of heterogeneous datasets, improving degradation modeling accuracy across varying cell mechanisms.

The remainder of this work is organized as follows: Section 2 outlines the proposed life evaluation method, including the integration of degradation modeling across varying battery operating conditions and cell mechanisms. Section 3 outlines the experimental design and evaluates model performance through ablation and comparative experiments. Section 4 offers detailed discussions of the life evaluation results. Section 5 summarizes the key findings of this work, highlighting the contributions and potential future directions for research in battery life evaluations.

In this study, a stress-informed transfer learning method across varying operating conditions and cell mechanisms is proposed to achieve efficient and accurate accelerated life evaluation for newly developed batteries. The schematic framework of the proposed method is illustrated in Fig. 1 , and a brief introduction of each process is described as follows:

First, battery degradation modeling across different operating conditions utilizes degradation data from higher-stress and normal-stress newly developed batteries as input. The primary objective of this section is to develop a model that evaluates battery cycle life in relation to stress intensity. The output consists of feature values that reflect the degradation trends influenced by operating conditions.

Second, battery degradation modeling across different cell mechanisms leverages heterogeneous datasets from batteries with varying mechanisms as input. This section employs domain-adaptive fine-tuning to enhance feature representation, thereby improving cycle life predictability for newly developed batteries. The output is a set of feature values related to battery degradation.

Finally, a model fusion methodology is employed to integrate the feature values derived from the previous sections. By iteratively updating the extracted features values, the hybrid model delivers a comprehensive and precise evaluation of the cycle life of newly developed batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery degradation modeling across different operating conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In the R&D phase, degradation testing for newly developed batteries typically involves batteries running under different load profiles, corresponding to varying operating conditions. Consequently, batteries subjected to higher stress are expected to fail more quickly, providing comprehensive degradation data across their entire cycle life. The objective of this section is to introduce a stress-informed battery degradation modeling method across different operating conditions, which enhances model quality for normal-stress batteries by integrating comprehensive degradation data collected from high-stress batteries in parallel testing.

In the proposed method, the challenge of unclear degradation trends in batteries operating under normal stress conditions is addressed by initially training a geometric Brownian motion (GBM) model using data collected from batteries subjected to high-stress conditions. This approach enables the model to effectively capture the inherent nonlinearity characteristic of the battery degradation process [25]. A model migration technique is then applied, transferring parameters from the high-stress model to normal-stress batteries through a slope and bias correction with the initial degradation segments. This transfer process integrates comprehensive degradation data from high-stress batteries into the modeling framework, allowing the model to adapt effectively to the unique nonlinear degradation patterns of normal-stress batteries. The complete framework of the proposed method is illustrated in Fig. 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: GBM model parameter identification with high-stress battery data
ce:section.ce:para: Geometric Brownian motion is a continuous-time stochastic process where the growth rate of a random variable in each time period is proportional to its current value and follows a normal distribution. When establishing degradation models for nonlinear systems, GBM is preferred over the more frequently used Wiener process because it ensures that degradation feature value do not become negative or unreasonably large [26]. This makes GBM a more physically realistic degradation process model and better suited for equivalent modeling of the degradation process in lithium-ion batteries.

The model obtains cycle life prediction results by estimating the number of cycles remaining until the battery's degradation feature reaches a predefined failure threshold. In this study, the degradation feature is defined as the normalized battery capacity observed during each charge and discharge cycle [27], represented as follows:
(1)
C
( t )
=
C a
p t
C a
p 0
× 100 %
where
C ( t )
denotes normalized battery capacity at cycle t ,
C a
p t
denotes measured battery capacity at cycle t , and
C a
p 0
denotes battery rated capacity.

The acquisition of the designed GBM model utilizes data from high-stress batteries in degradation testing. The predicted degradation feature value at each cycle is expressed as follows [28]:
(2)
C
( t )
=
C 0
exp
{
(
μ −
1 2
σ
2
)
t + σ
W t
}
where
C 0
represents the initial degradation feature and is equal to 1. μ and σ represent the drift and diffusion rate of the model, relating to the average degradation rate and fluctuation of the battery degradation feature, respectively.
W t
represents Brownian motion with variance t .

To describe the relationship between single stress factor S under different operating conditions and the average degradation rate μ , the inverse power law model is introduced and expressed as follows [16]:
(3)
μ = α
S
β
where α and β are parameters to be estimated. The principle of the inverse power law model is that the stress intensity has an active impact on the rate of system degradation, with higher stress corresponds to faster degradation rate.

To extend this model to account for multiple stress factors, we refer to relevant studies [29,30] and assume that the effects of stress factors such as temperature, discharge rate, and state of charge are multiplicative. The degradation rate can therefore be represented as follows:
(4)
μ = α
∏
k = 1
m
S k
β k
where m denote the total amount of different stress factors,
S k
and
β k
denote the k-th stress factor its corresponding stress exponent. The principle of the inverse power law model remains that higher stress intensity leads to faster system degradation. By adopting a multiplicative structure, the model reflects the synergistic influence of stress factors on the degradation process.

Replacing μ in formula (2) with the inverse power law model and we obtain the battery degradation model considering stress factors:
(5)
C
( t , S )

W t
}

The above formula includes three undetermined parameters α ,
β k
and σ . The maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) is adopted to estimate these unknown parameters. The likelihood function of the GBM model is shown as follows [28]:
(6)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model migration with normal-stress battery data
ce:section.ce:para: To establish a reliable framework for battery cycle life evaluation in relation to stress intensity, the GBM model serves as a foundational approach. However, the absence of complete degradation data under normal-stress conditions limits its accuracy in such scenarios. As a variant of transfer learning, model migration is commonly employed to address data incompleteness challenges during the model development process [21]. This technique is particularly advantageous for battery cycle life prediction using stochastic models. By introducing normal-stress battery data into the modeling process and applying adjustments to the stress factor, a migrated model can be constructed that preserves the robustness of the original high-stress framework while effectively adapting to normal-stress operating conditions.

Specifically, A slope and bias correction is applied to the stress factor of the base model to obtain a new model for normal-stress batteries [20]. This transformation allows the model to flexibly account for differences in degradation rates between stress regimes. Specifically, the stress factor S of the base model (5) is adjusted as follows:
(8)
S
n e w
= ω S +
ε
where ω and
ε
are slope and bias correction factors to be identified.

Then the migrated model for normal-stress battery cycle life prediction is obtained as follows:
(9)
C
( t ,
S k
n e w
)
= C
( t ,
ω k
S k
+
ε
k
)

{
(

∏

ω k

+
ε
k
)
−
β k
−
1 2
σ
2
)
t + σ
W t
}
where
ω k
and
ε
k
are unknown migration parameters of the model.

The unknown parameters in the model migration process are individually estimated with data from each normal-stress battery in degradation testing through least squares estimation (LSE). LSE yields estimation results through minimizing the residual sum of squares
R S S
shown as follows:
(10)
R S S

To further facilitate iterative prediction of battery degradation feature in the subsequent model fusion section, an equivalent form of the non-iterative stochastic model (9) is obtained as follows:
(12)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery degradation modeling across different cell mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: In real industrial scenarios, extensive degradation data from fully deteriorated historical battery models are available. This abundance of historical data enables the creation of highly accurate base models for battery life evaluations. This section aims to introduce a method for battery degradation modeling across different cell mechanisms. By leveraging comprehensive degradation data from historical model batteries, the effectiveness of degradation modeling for newly developed batteries with slight differences in cell mechanisms can be significantly enhanced.

The proposed methodology begins with establishing a Transformer-based base model trained on historical battery data to capture intrinsic degradation patterns of lithium-ion batteries. Domain adaptive fine-tuning is then applied to the base model, enabling the transfer of essential feature representations to new battery systems, even with limited degradation features. This transfer learning process accounts for the subtle differences in the cell mechanisms between historical and new model batteries, enabling the model to adapt to the specific characteristics of newly developed batteries. The complete framework of the proposed battery degradation modeling across different cell mechanisms is illustrated in Fig. 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transformer-based model training with historical model battery data
ce:section.ce:para: The Transformer network is a type of neural network based on self-attention mechanisms. Its most significant feature lies in its capability in capturing and analyzing long-range data features, ensuring that important information is not overlooked even when dealing with large input segments [31]. This makes Transformer network well-suited for battery degradation modeling tasks.

The Transformer encoder architecture used in the proposed method takes historical battery degradation features as input and outputs the predicted degradation trajectory. Before the historical battery degradation feature sequences are fed into the network, they undergo preprocessing steps including input embedding and positional encoding. Input embedding involves transforming raw capacity data into vectors of a specified dimension suitable for neural network processing. This transformation is done using an embedding layer which maps each input token to a high-dimensional space. Once the input data sequences are embedded, the positional encoding mechanism is applied to indicate the position of data carrying relevant information within an input sequence. Typically, this encoding is achieved using sine and cosine functions, as shown as (13) and (14):
(13)
P E
( p o s , 2 i )
= sin
( p o s /
10000
2 i / dim
)
(14)
P E
( p o s , 2 i + 1 )
= cos
( p o s /
10000
2 i / dim
)
where
p o s
denotes the position of individual feature value, dim corresponds to data dimension,
2 i
and
2 i + 1
are used to differentiate between even and odd positions.

The Transformer encoder processes the sequence resulting from previous steps for further encoding. This encoding step leverages the multi-head self-attention mechanism shown in Fig. 4 , which is crucial for identifying and capturing correlations between sequence data over varying distances. First, within the multi-head self-attention layer, the Q (Query), K (Key), and V (Value) components are computed using the battery degradation feature input matrix and weight matrices, depicted as (15):
(15)
{
Q =
W Q
×
A t
K =
W K
×
A t
V =
W V
×
A t
where,
W Q
W K
and
W V
are corresponding weight matrices,
A t
denote the battery degradation feature input matrix at cycle t .

Subsequently, these components are used to calculate the attention weights
A t t n
for an individual head, shown as (16):
(16)
A t t n
(
Q , K , V
)
= V × softmax
(
K
T
Q
d l
)
where
d l
is a parameter set to prevent gradients from becoming too small.

After obtaining attention weights for multiple heads, they are combined to obtain the final multi-head attention weights. The process can be expressed as (17):
(17)
M u l t i h e a d
(
Q , K , V
)
=
[
A t t n
(
Q 1
,
K 1
,
V 1
)
. . .
A t t n
(
Q n
,
K n
,
V n
)
]
W 0
where
W 0
is a set of trainable weights.

Subsequently, the feed-forward network is employed to process and refine the outputs derived from the multi-head attention mechanism. Specifically, the feed-forward network takes as input the set of multi-head attention weights and applies a series of non-linear transformations. These transformations are pivotal in enhancing the network's capability to discern complex patterns and relationships within the data, thereby facilitating more precise and accurate predictions. The computation process is presented as (18):
(18)
MA = M u l t i h e a d
(
Q , K , V
)
FF
( MA )
= ReLU
(
A
W 1
+
B 1
)
W 2
+
B 2
where
W 1
,
W 2
,
B 1
and
B 2
are trainable weight and bias parameters.

The next feature value is predicted by mapping feed-forward results with a set of fully connected layers, shown as (19):
(19)
H = F F
( MA )
C
(
t + 1
)
= g
(
W 3
H +
B 3
)
where H denotes the hidden feature output by the feed-forward layer,
C (
t + 1
)
denotes the predicted feature value for the next cycle,
g ( ⋅ )
denotes the mapping function,
W 3
and
B 3
are trainable weight and bias parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Domain adaptive fine-tuning with newly designed battery data
ce:section.ce:para: Fine-tuning is a crucial process in machine learning that adjusts the parameters of a pre-trained model to better fit a specific task or domain. By making small adjustments to the parameters of a model pretrained on source domain data, model performance on target domain can be greatly improved. However, insufficient target degradation features resulting from data incompleteness pose a significant challenge, potentially undermining the model's evaluation capabilities. Domain-adaptive fine-tuning alleviates distribution mismatches across domains, supporting more consistent and transferable feature learning [32]. By reducing discrepancies in data distribution across battery datasets with differing cell mechanisms, this approach facilitates better alignment of feature representations between the source and target domains. The resulting improvement in feature transferability compensates for the lack of degradation features, thereby enhancing model performance across diverse battery mechanisms [33].

As a popular method to measure the data distribution shift between different datasets, MMD is crucial for identifying and quantifying discrepancies between the source and target domains, ensuring that the fine-tuning process effectively aligns their distributions. MMD operates on the principle of comparing the mean embeddings of the source and target data distributions in a high-dimensional reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS). Mathematically, MMD is defined as the distance between the mean embeddings of the two distributions. Suppose m extracted feature samples from the source domain input follow distribution
D s
, and n extracted feature samples from the target domain input follows distribution
D t
, then the squared MMD distance between distribution
D s
and
D t
is represented as [22]:
(20)
MMD
2
(
D s
,
D t
)
=
∥
1 m
∑
i = 1
m
ϕ
(
s i S
)
−
1 n
∑
j = 1
n
ϕ
(
s j T
)
∥
H 2
where
s i S
and
s j T
denote individual extracted feature sample in
D s
and
D t
, respectively. The expression
∥ ⋅ ∥
H
denotes norm in the RKHS. The function
ϕ ( ⋅ )
represents the mapping function between the input space and the RKHS. Due to the challenges in explicitly defining high-dimensional mappings for data in the input space, the kernel trick
k
(
s S
,
s T
)
=
〈
ϕ
(
s S
)
, ϕ
(
s T
)
〉
H
is employed to simplify the computation using a kernel function, shown as follows:
(21)
MMD
2
(
D s
,
D t
)
=
1
m
2
∑
i = 1
m
∑
j = 1
m
k
(
s i S
,
s j S
)
+
1
n
2
∑
i = 1
n
∑
j = 1
n
k
(
s i T
,
s j T
)
−
2
m n
∑
i = 1
m
∑
j = 1
n
k
(
s i S
,
s j T
)
where
k ( ⋅ )
represents the Gaussian kernel function with bandwidth parameter ζ , expressed as follows:
(22)
k
(
s S
,
s T
)
= exp
(
−
∥
s S
−
s T
∥
2
2
ζ
2
)

In the proposed domain adaptive fine-tuning process, data from historical model batteries and newly designed batteries are treated as the source and target domains, respectively. The newly designed battery data collected in degradation testing is used to fine-tune the model parameters in the two fully connected layers responsible for the model output. This fine-tuning helps in adapting the model to the specific characteristics of the new battery data. The total loss function is designed as the summation of prediction loss
L Pred
and MMD loss
L
M M D
, shown as follows:
(23)
L
P r e d
=
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
(
C i
^
−
C i
)
2
L
M M D
=
MMD
2
(
D s
,
D t
)
L
T o t a l
=
L
P r e d
+ γ
L
M M D
where
L Pred
is defined as the root mean square error computed between predicted feature value
C i
^
output by the second fully connected layer and true feature value
C i
.
L
M M D
is defined as the MMD distance between the extracted feature values of the source and target domains, both output by the first fully connected layer. γ denotes a weight coefficient and is set to be 0.1 during the fine-tuning process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fusion model development and life evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: One primary motivation of this work is to achieve the integration of battery life evaluation models to address the critical challenge posed by the unavailability of complete degradation data for newly developed lithium-ion batteries. By integrating multi-modal degradation data from high-stress-condition batteries, which exhibit faster degradation trends, and historical model batteries with similar but not identical cell mechanisms, the hybrid model effectively compensates for data incompleteness. Additionally, it surpasses the inherent limitations of individual models, thereby enhancing the accuracy and reliability of cycle life predictions.
The schematic diagram of the fusion model development and life evaluation is shown in Fig. 5 :

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model fusion with particle filter algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: As a subset of sequential Monte Carlo methods, particle filter has emerged as a powerful tool for state estimation in non-linear and non-Gaussian systems. It operates by recursively approximating the posterior distribution of the system's state using a set of random samples, known as particles, each associated with a weight that reflects its likelihood. Particle filter has found extensive use in the field of lithium-ion degradation modeling due to its ability to facilitate the fusion of multiple models or data sources [34,35]. This capability enables more accurate predictions by capturing a comprehensive range of aspects related to battery health degradation.

To achieve model fusion of the models under different operating conditions and cell mechanisms, and to achieve life evaluation using the particle filter algorithm, the state-space model is first established using the following two equations:
(24)
x t
= f
(
x
t − 1
,
w t
)
(25)
y t
= h
(
x t
,
v t
)
where
x t
and
y t
represent the system state and measurement at cycle t , respectively. The functions
f ( ⋅ )
and
h ( ⋅ )
denote the state transition function and measurement function. The state transition function is derived from the model across different operating conditions, capturing how the state evolves under varying operational stresses. The measurement function, on the other hand, is derived from the model across different cell mechanisms, reflecting how the measurements relate to the state under various chemical compositions.
w k
and
v k
represent the process and measurement noise, which are both independent of time.

Consequently, the predictive battery degradation feature is iteratively updated through the following steps. The first step is to initialize a set of particles. Each particle represents a possible state of the system and is initialized by sampling from a normal distribution, reflecting the prior uncertainty about the state. Given the initial state
x t
and the number of particles n , particles are sampled as follows:
(26)
x t i
∼ N
(
x t
,
Σ
)
, i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n
where
N ( ⋅ )
denotes the normal distribution, and
Σ
is the covariance matrix representing the uncertainty in the initial state.

After initializing the particles, the next step involves calculating the weight of each particle. These weights are based on the likelihood of the observed measurements given the particle's state. The weight calculation ensures that particles which better explain the observed data are given higher importance. Mathematically, the weight
w t i
of the i -th particle at time t is computed using:
(27)
w t i
= p
(
y t
|
x t i
)
, i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n

The weights are normalized by:
(28)
w t
′
i
=
w t i
∑
j = 1
n
w t j
,
i = 1 , 2 , … , n

To avoid the problem of particle degeneracy, where a few particles have significant weights while others have negligible weights, a resampling step is introduced. Resampling involves generating a new set of particles by sampling with replacement from the current set of particles according to their normalized weights. This step ensures that particles with higher weights are more likely to be chosen, effectively focusing computational resources on the most likely states. The resampling process generates a new set of particles
{
x t
* i
}
i = 1
n
with function
R S
shown as follows:
(29)
{
x t
* i
}
i = 1
n
= R S
(
{
x t i
,
w t
′
i
}
i = 1
n
)

Finally, the state of the system is estimated based on the resampled particles. The estimated state
x ^
t
at cycle t can be computed as the weighted average of the particle states:
(30)
x ^
t
=
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
x t
* i

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation feature iterative prediction and life evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: The fusion model provides iterative predictions of battery degradation features by continuously updating state estimates as new data becomes available. To integrate predictions from models trained under varying operating conditions and chemical mechanisms, a particle filter is employed as a probabilistic fusion framework. At each cycle, the model incorporates new information about the battery's performance and updates the state estimates of the degradation feature. If the predicted feature value does not reach battery EOL, it becomes the input for the next iteration, repeating the prediction process. The fusion process operates independently of the training and parameter optimization of the constituent GBM and Transformer models. Rather than modifying model internals, the particle filter aggregates SOH trend predictions to refine the degradation feature estimate over time. This design ensures modularity and preserves the learning dynamics of individual predictors, allowing the fusion layer to remain model-agnostic and adaptable across heterogeneous modeling sources.

The cycle life of the battery is determined by the first hitting time, defined as the point when the degradation feature reaches a predefined failure threshold [36], shown as follows:
(31)
C L = inf { t : C
( t )
≤ θ | C
(
t 0
)
≥ θ }
where θ is a predefined failure threshold,
t 0
denote the cycle at which prediction starts.

In this study, the failure threshold is set at 80 % of the initial degradation feature. Therefore, the battery is considered to have reached its EOL when its feature value drops to 80 %. If during any iterative prediction cycle the feature value meets or falls below this threshold, the prediction process stops, signaling that the battery has reached its EOL.

The first dataset utilized in this study, herein referred to as the HIT dataset, was collected over a six-month accelerated degradation test. This dataset encompasses battery aging data from five independent test channels, each subjected to distinct load profiles. All 18,650 batteries used in the testing were manufactured by DLG (Shanghai) Battery Co., Ltd., with a nominal capacity of 2200 mAh and a cell chemistry of Li(NiCoMn)O2.The test bench incorporates a BTS3000n battery testing system produced by Neware (Shenzhen) Co., Ltd., which measures parameters such as voltage, current, and temperature throughout the testing process. An environmental chamber developed by Dongguan Bell Experiment Equipment Co., Ltd., was employed to maintain a controlled temperature of 25 °C. Data from all tests were collected via a host computer [37].

Within the HIT dataset, battery cells are labeled as Cx-y, where x denotes the channel index and y denotes the cell index. All cells were charged and discharged between terminal voltages of 4.2 V and 2.7 V, respectively. Channels 1, 2, and 3 share a uniform charge rate of 0.5C, with discharge rates differentiated as 0.5C, 1.0C, and 1.5C, respectively. Additionally, channels 1, 4, and 5 share a uniform discharge rate of 0.5C, while their charge rates vary as 0.5C, 1.0C, and 1.5C, respectively. Charge and discharge rates were selected as experimental variables due to their higher sensitivity to battery degradation compared to other factors [38]. Temperature was excluded from the testing criteria, as space batteries typically operate under stable thermal conditions.

The second dataset comprises aging data from four prismatic batteries, provided by the Center for Advanced Life Cycle Engineering (CALCE) at the University of Maryland [39]. These prismatic batteries have a nominal capacity of 1100 mAh and a cell chemistry of LiCoO2. The batteries were tested using an Arbin Battery Tester. The experimental setup for the CALCE dataset included charge and discharge terminal voltages of 4.2 V and 2.7 V, and charge and discharge rates of 1C.

Capacity degradation of battery cells in the two datasets are illustrated in Fig. 6 . The degradation curves of four battery cells in CALCE dataset is shown in Fig. 6(a), and the degradation curves of four battery cells from each channel in HIT dataset are displayed in Fig. 6(b). A summary of experimental details of both battery datasets are summarized in Table 1 .

Data preprocessing is targeted at the normalized capacity degradation data for cells in both the HIT and CALCE datasets. Outliers are identified and removed using the interquartile range method, with extreme values replaced by the mean of nearby data points. To reduce noise and emphasize underlying trends, spline smoothing is applied using cubic spline functions, which fit smooth, piecewise-defined curves through the original feature values. This process results in a refined and cleaner representation of the data, effectively reducing noise and highlighting the underlying trends in the degradation feature values.

To assess the accuracy of cycle life evaluation, three key metrics are utilized: absolute error (AE), mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE). AE represents the absolute difference between the predicted and actual cycle life values for an individual battery cell, providing a fundamental measure of prediction accuracy. MAE quantifies the average AE between the predicted and actual cycle life values, and RMSE normalizes the error by emphasizing larger deviations, offering a more weighted evaluation. In Chapter 3, where the HIT dataset is used to evaluate individual battery cells, AE is adopted to report performance concisely. In contrast, Chapter 4 involves broader analyses across multiple battery cells from diverse test datasets; therefore, MAE and RMSE are employed to ensure consistency and enable robust comparative evaluation. The three metrics are calculated as follows:
(32)
A E =
|
C
L
p r e d
− C
L
t r u e
|
(33)
M A E =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
|
C
L i
p r e d
− C
L i
t r u e
|
(34)
R M S E =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
(
C
L i
p r e d
− C
L i
t r u e
)
2
where
C
L
p r e d
denote the predicted cycle life value,
C
L
t r u e
denote true cycle life value, n denote the total number of battery cells evaluated.

The proposed stress-informed transfer learning method is further assessed alongside two mainstream data-insensitive battery degradation modeling methods. The proposed stress-informed transfer learning method is further assessed alongside two state-of-the-art battery degradation modeling baselines. The first baseline, referred to as Benchmark1, is a method based on extensive feature selection and a domain-adaptive network architecture [40]. Handcrafted and extracted features are concatenated and fed into an alignment CNN, trained with a composite loss function combining mean squared error and CORAL loss to enhance domain alignment. The second baseline, referred to as Benchmark2, incorporates physical constraints into a data-driven framework to improve the realism of degradation predictions [41,42]. Based on manually selected features, this model embeds physics-based priors into the training objective alongside prediction accuracy, thereby enforcing physically consistent degradation behavior and improving the plausibility of the predicted life trajectories.

The training data employed across the battery modeling methods in the experimental sections, as depicted in Fig. 7 , is categorized into three primary datasets. Partial HIT Channel 1 data represents newly designed batteries with incomplete degradation segments. HIT high-stress channels, including channel 2, 3,4 and 5, capture batteries subjected to higher-stress conditions. Finally, the CALCE dataset incorporates batteries with contrasting cell mechanisms.

The ablation experiment involves methods within the stress-informed transfer learning framework, including GBM, MM-GBM, TFM, FT-TFM, and Fusion. For GBM, the model is directly trained on target domain cells using partial HIT Channel 1 data. MM-GBM trains the model on source domain cells from the high-stress channels and subsequently applies a model migration technique to adapt it to the target domain. TFM employs a Transformer model trained exclusively on the target domain. FT-TFM involves a Transformer model pre-trained on source domain cells from CALCE, followed by domain adaptive fine-tuning for the target domain. Finally, the Fusion method integrates models from MM-GBM and FT-TFM using a particle filter algorithm, synthesizing data from both source and target domains to enhance predictive accuracy and robustness.

The comparison experiment evaluates the proposed method alongside its counterparts, Benchmark1 and Benchmark2. Benchmark1 is developed using data from batteries with the same cell mechanisms from HIT high-stress channels, whereas Benchmark2 incorporates a broader dataset comprising HIT Channels 1 through 5.

The testing phase for the methods utilizes the remaining data segments from HIT Channel 1 to evaluate performance. This dataset comprises degradation data excluded from the training process, ensuring a robust assessment of predictive accuracy. Performance is measured using the AE metric for individual battery cells, while MAE and RMSE metrics are applied to target cell batches.

The ablation experiment is designed to assess the overall performance of individual components within the stress-informed transfer learning method. Cycle life estimation results across five model training strategies, each utilizing varied lengths of training data, are systematically compared. Due to space constraints, this section focuses exclusively on the cycle life evaluation results for the C1–1 battery cell. Among the stochastic process-based models discussed in this section, GBM and MM-GBM each contain 4 and 8 parameters, with MM-GBM introducing 4 additional migration parameters beyond those in GBM. Table 2 summarizes the models incorporating neural network structures. TFM and FT-TFM share an identical architecture but are trained on different datasets. The total parameter count for the Fusion method combines the parameters from FT-TFM and MM-GBM.

The results of cycle life predictions are illustrated in Fig. 8 , while the performance of these methods is evaluated using the AE metric, as presented in Table 3 . In cases where an approach fails to generate cycle life predictions, the AE metric is computed by subtracting the prediction start cycle from the actual cycle count.

As shown in Table 3, both GBM and TFM exhibited limited generalization capability under data-scarce conditions, with notable performance degradation at shorter training data lengths. This highlights their dependence on abundant and complete datasets for accurate cycle life prediction. In contrast, MM-GBM and FT-TFM, which incorporate transfer learning strategies, achieved significantly improved results. MM-GBM applies a model migration approach, leveraging complete degradation trajectories from high-stress batteries to guide the learning process. This enables the model to capture comprehensive aging patterns that are otherwise difficult to extract from limited target data. FT-TFM, on the other hand, adapts pre-trained representations derived from diverse cell mechanisms to the target domain, enhancing predictive performance with limited data availability.

The fusion strategy yielded the highest overall performance by integrating predictions from MM-GBM and FT-TFM using a particle filter algorithm. This approach mitigates individual model biases and enhances robustness through probabilistic aggregation. These results reaffirm the effectiveness of multi-modal integration in addressing challenges posed by data scarcity and variability in battery degradation modeling.

In addition to performance evaluation, we further analyze the training time of each model to assess computational complexity. As shown in Fig. 9 , GBM and MM-GBM exhibit significantly longer training durations than the neural network models, particularly as input sequence length increases. Compared to GBM, MM-GBM incurs additional cost due to correction parameter estimation from high-stress trajectories. In contrast, TFM and FT-TFM maintain relatively stable training durations across data lengths, owing to their fixed architecture and efficient parallelization. The Fusion model combines MM-GBM and FT-TFM outputs via particle filtering, resulting in the highest overall training time but delivering the most robust predictions.

The comparison experiment assesses the proposed stress-informed transfer learning method alongside mainstream data-insensitive battery degradation modeling methods. The analysis focuses on the cycle life predictive accuracy of the proposed method in contrast to the established methodologies, evaluating method performance in coping with data incompleteness during the early stages of battery development. This section also focuses on the results for the C1–1 battery cell due to space limitations. The cycle life evaluation results are visualized in Fig 10 , with method performance assessed using AE metric, as detailed in Table 4 . Notably, Benchmark1 does not model the battery degradation trajectory but instead directly outputs cycle life estimation results, which are represented by pentagon markers in the figures.

It can be observed from Table 4 that Benchmark1 demonstrates moderate predictive performance across varying training data lengths, with relatively consistent behavior compared to other methods. Its most favorable results are observed at 10 % and 20 % data lengths, indicating a degree of early-stage adaptability. Nevertheless, the absolute error remains substantial in these low-data regimes, which limits its practical applicability in scenarios where only minimal degradation information is available.

Benchmark2 demonstrates a distinct performance trajectory, characterized by elevated prediction errors at shorter training data lengths. From 30 % onward, its error decreases progressively with increasing data availability. This pattern suggests that Benchmark2 is sensitive to data quantity and requires sufficient degradation information to achieve reliable accuracy, thereby limiting its robustness in data-constrained scenarios.

The proposed stress-informed transfer learning method demonstrates a balanced performance across various training data lengths. The model shows satisfactory results as the training data length increases to 30 %, further improving its prediction accuracy with additional training data. Notably, the method consistently outperforms the benchmark methods at medium training data lengths, showcasing its superior accuracy in battery degradation modeling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of life prediction performance across method types
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the methods applied in the ablation and comparison experiments are utilized to evaluate the cycle life of four battery cells from the HIT channel 1 dataset. The subsequent discussion highlights the predictive performance of each method, with MAE and RMSE results detailed in Fig. 11 and Table 5 .

As observed from Table 5, GBM exhibited a gradual improvement as training data increased. MM-GBM, which incorporates model migration, consistently outperformed GBM across all data lengths. TFM struggled with poor performance at shorter data lengths, but began to show improvement as the training data increased. In comparison, FT-TFM displayed superior accuracy at medium and longer data lengths, though errors at 70 % are higher than most other approaches. This may be attributed to overfitting during the domain-adaptive fine-tuning process, where excessive specialization compromised predictive generalization for certain datasets.

The Fusion method emerged as the most robust approach, achieving low evaluation errors across data lengths ranging from 30 % to 70 %. At short training data lengths of 10 % and 20 %, prediction errors are relatively high due to limited input information. Despite this limitation, the Fusion method's multi-modal integration enabled it to outperform other approaches consistently.

Regarding the comparison methods, Benchmark1 demonstrates strong performance at shorter training data lengths, benefiting from its reliance on handcrafted features and domain-adaptive architecture that enables effective representation learning under limited data conditions. However, its feature extraction and alignment mechanisms may lack the flexibility to capture increasingly complex degradation dynamics, limiting its scalability in data-rich scenarios. In contrast, Benchmark2 exhibits a delayed but pronounced improvement in prediction accuracy. While its performance remains poor shorter data length, it begins to improve gradually thereafter, reaching high accuracy at 70 %. This trend highlights Benchmark2’s reliance on sufficient degradation information to fully exploit its embedded physical constraints and deliver physically consistent predictions.

For method robustness analysis, Fig. 11 reveals pronounced fluctuations in model outputs at 20 % and 30 % training data lengths. These inconsistencies are primarily attributed to partial prediction failures—some methods successfully generate cycle life estimates for certain battery cells, while fail to produce usable results for others cells. After excluding these anomalous cases, Benchmark1, Benchmark2, and the Fusion method exhibit relatively high average prediction stability across the evaluated data lengths. In contrast, GBM and TFM demonstrate the most pronounced instability, highlighting the critical role of transfer learning techniques in improving model robustness when training data is limited.

In summary, transfer learning methods consistently outperformed the base models, showcasing enhanced adaptability under incomplete data scenarios. While Benchmark1 and Benchmark2 displayed strengths in specific data regimes, the proposed stress-informed transfer learning method achieved optimal performance between 30 % to 70 % training data lengths, reaffirming its versatility and reliability for battery degradation modeling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relations between prediction accuracy and training data length
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section, the relationship between prediction accuracy and training data length is analyzed for the methods applied in the ablation and comparison experiments. The subsequent discussion provides an examination of the performance of each method at specific training data lengths. The cycle life prediction results for each method, evaluated across varied training data lengths, are illustrated in Fig. 12 and Fig. 13
.

As observed, most methods show improved evaluation accuracy with increasing training data length. At shorter data lengths of 10 % and 20 %, Benchmark1 is the only method capable of generating cycle life predictions for all battery cells. However, its error rates remain prohibitively high, limiting its practical applicability in early-stage scenarios. From 30 % onward, the Fusion method demonstrates clear superiority. At 30 %, it achieves the lowest error among all methods, with a MAE of 29.00 and RMSE of 33.02, significantly outperforming the benchmark approaches. Between 40 % and 70 %, the Fusion method consistently maintains its leading position, with MAE values ranging from 25.50 to 21.50 and RMSE values from 28.16 to 23.11. Benchmark2 shows gradual improvement across increasing data lengths, ultimately reaching competitive accuracy at 70 %. In contrast, Benchmark1 stabilizes at moderate error levels beyond 30 %, reflecting limited scalability despite its early-stage adaptability.

In terms of convergence behavior for the transfer learning methods, the MM-GBM method requires at least 40 % of the normal-stress degradation trajectory to achieve stable and accurate predictions, indicating a relatively slow adaptation to limited data conditions. In contrast, the FT-TFM method demonstrates reliable evaluation performance with only 30 % training data, as evidenced by a marked increase in ΔMAE and ΔRMSE values at this threshold. Notably, the Fusion method also exhibits a pronounced performance surge at 30 %, emphasizing its robust evaluation capability with as little as 30 % degradation trajectory. Beyond 30 %, the method demonstrates slower improvement, with even negative trends observed from 40 % to 70 %, possibly attributed to the accumulation of data noise that hinders effective feature learning. Nevertheless, when compared to the best-performing benchmark method, Benchmark2, the improvements in MAE and RMSE at 30 % training data length are quantified as 63.40 % and 58.55 %, respectively. This highlights the proposed stress-informed transfer learning method's potential for reliable battery life evaluation in scenarios where minimal training data is available.

This study introduces a stress-informed transfer learning framework for the accelerated life evaluation of newly developed lithium-ion batteries. By leveraging comprehensive degradation data from high-stress-condition batteries in parallel testing and historical model batteries with variations in cell mechanisms, the method enhances cycle life evaluation accuracy. A stochastic model is employed to capture nonlinear degradation dynamics, while model migration adapts to unique trends under varying stresses. Additionally, a Transformer-based model facilitates cross-mechanism modeling, and domain-adaptive fine-tuning improves feature representation. By integrating these models developed with multi-modal data, the stress-informed transfer learning model achieves a 63.40 %, improvement in MAE and a 58.55 % improvement in RMSE at 30 % training data length compared to mainstream data-insensitive methods. This demonstrates the strong potential of the proposed framework for accelerating battery life evaluation of newly developed cells. Within the development cycle of space lithium-ion batteries—where typical qualification requires years of nominal life testing—the method can be positioned as an early-stage screening and assessment tool. It complements conventional accelerated life testing protocols for evaluating battery cycle life. By reducing the dependency on completing full accelerated life testing cycles, the approach provides actionable insights during initial verification phases, supporting earlier design decisions while maintaining compatibility with established qualification standards.

For future research, this study has focused on space application scenarios, where batteries operate under stable thermal conditions. Consequently, the impact of environmental temperature variations was not considered in the experimental design. To broaden the applicability of the proposed framework to other battery use cases, such as electric vehicles and drones, it will be essential to incorporate environmental temperature as a key operating parameter. Additionally, a multi-factor orthogonal experiment, accounting for stress factors such as temperature, charge and discharge rates, and terminal voltages, will be conducted to enable more comprehensive modeling. Moreover, addressing the substantial errors observed with shorter training data lengths will require exploring the integration of mechanism-based methodologies. Such an approach has the potential to reduce dependence on extensive datasets while enhancing the accuracy and reliability of accelerated life evaluation models.

Guangyuan Zeng: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology. Yuhang Du: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation. Yuchen Song: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Datong Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yu Peng: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Yuchen Song reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No 62201177, No 62371168, and Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province under Grant No YQ2023F006.

Energy AI

IEEE Trans Instrum Meas

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lyu G., Zhang H., Miao Q. RUL prediction of lithium-ion battery in early-cycle stage based on similar sample fusion under Lebesgue sampling framework. IEEE Trans Instrum Meas, 2023, 72: 1–11.
